The patient is a 53-year-old female with a complex history of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) and cirrhosis who was admitted for recurrent cholangitis. 
 I will synthesize the diagnostic findings from ultrasound, MRCP, and ERCP, the procedural outcome of the biliary sweep, and the pharmacological management involving the escalation to Meropenem. 
 The summary will cover the final diagnoses, key clinical evidence, and the discharge plan including home IV antibiotics and follow-up requirements.
 Significant Results and Diagnostic Information: This 53-year-old female with Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC), cirrhosis (Childs B, MELD 12), and portal hypertension presented with right upper quadrant (RUQ) abdominal pain and fever (100.7°F).
 Diagnostic imaging (RUQ US and MRCP) identified biliary sludge and choledocholithiasis in the distal common bile duct (CBD), along with a stone in the cystic duct remnant. 
 Admission labs showed elevated liver enzymes (Alk Phos 308, ALT 46, AST 56) and coagulopathy (INR 1.3). 
 An ERCP was performed, which confirmed narrowed hepatic ducts consistent with PSC and successfully removed scant sludge via balloon sweep. 
 An EGD revealed an 8 mm ulcer at the gastroesophageal junction and portal gastropathy without active varices. 
 Clinical Course and Discharge Summary: The patient's diagnosis was recurrent cholangitis. 
 Due to a history of multi-drug resistant infections, her antibiotic regimen was escalated from Ertapenem to Meropenem (1000 mg IV every 8 hours).
 Following the ERCP and antibiotic adjustment, she became afebrile and her symptoms improved. 
 She was discharged in stable condition to continue IV Meropenem via a PICC line at home, finishing one week post-ERCP. 
 Discharge medications include maintenance therapies for cirrhosis (Ursodiol, Rifaximin, Lactulose, Cholestyramine).
 She is scheduled for follow-up blood work (CBC, LFTs, BUN, Creatinine) and must reschedule her outpatient MRI and colonoscopy through the transplant clinic.
